🚨 You could be missing out on thousands in pension tax relief, and most people don’t even realise it.

If you’re a basic rate taxpayer, your pension contributions automatically get a 20% boost.
That means if you want £100 in your pension, you only pay £80, the government tops up the rest.
BUT 👀

If you’re a higher or additional rate taxpayer, that extra 20–25% relief is NOT automatic.
You have to actively claim it by ticking the right box when you file your tax return.
Example👇

Higher rate taxpayer aiming for £100 in your pension?
It could actually cost you just £60, but only if you claim it.

So be honest… did you tick the box? ✅

If pensions confuse you (you’re not alone), drop a comment or check it in the app, future you will thank you 💸📈

£525 million in revenue.
£0 in corporation tax.
Last year, Starbucks reportedly paid 0% in UK corporation tax, not because they broke the rules, but because of how profits were structured.

By paying tens of millions in royalty fees to a US entity within the group, their UK business recorded a loss on paper. And when a company reports a loss… there’s no corporation tax to pay.

It’s legal. It’s strategic.
But it’s also why many people choose to shop independent, because local businesses don’t have complex international structures. They pay tax here, in the UK, just like everyday taxpayers.

So here’s the real question:
Do you think this is smart business… or does it highlight a bigger issue in the tax system?
Let us know your thoughts below 👇

#TaxNews #CorporationTax #UKTax #TaxExplained #MoneyMatters

✈️ Holiday booked? Don’t book yourself a tax problem.

If you’re a sole trader and think you can expense any holiday as a business cost… this one’s for you 👀

HMRC is very clear: holidays are not allowable business expenses because they’re not wholly and exclusively for work. And yes — this is one of the most common traps they catch people out on 🚨

Now, here’s the part most people miss 👇
If you travel abroad and attend a work meeting, conference, or business event, you may be able to expense only the business-related portion of that trip. Not the beach days. Not the cocktails. Just the work part.

Bottom line?
Trying to expense a holiday as a business cost could cost you far more in the long run 💸

You get £20,000 EVERY tax year to save or invest completely tax-free.

✔️ No tax on savings interest

So whether you’re saving, investing, or doing both, an ISA is one of the easiest ways to legally pay less tax.

Skipping your tax return doesn’t mean skipping tax.It actually means HMRC decides for you, and it’s rarely in your favour.If you don’t file your Self Assessment, HMRC can issue something called a determination.This is an estimated tax bill based on what they think you earned for the year.The problem?These estimates often use outdated information, ignore expenses, and massively overstate what you owe.That’s why so many people panic, believing they owe far more tax than they actually do.Here’s what HMRC doesn’t explain clearly:You can’t appeal a determination, but you can replace it by filing your actual tax return.Once you submit your real figures, the determination is wiped out and replaced with the correct tax bill.It’s not a punishment, it’s a pressure tactic to get returns submitted.The takeaway:File your return.Claim your expenses.Don’t let HMRC guess your income.Want more tax facts HMRC doesn’t explain?Comment “pie” and I’ll send you the direct link to my free tax-saving newsletter.#SelfAssessment #HMRC #TaxTips #UKTax #SmallBusinessUK #FreelancerUK #MoneyTalks #TaxApp #FinanceReels

"Think HMRC can just take money straight from your bank? 😱 Not quite! They can use something called direct recovery of debts—but only if you owe over £1000, the debt is overdue, and you’ve ignored repeated contact.
Even then, they cannot leave you with nothing, skip steps, or surprise you out of the blue. 💡 They must:
Contact you multiple times
Offer payment plans
Give a final warning
Allow you to challenge it
If you’re struggling with tax debt, don’t panic! Time to pay plans are there to protect you. Speak to HMRC—they’d rather work with you than take extreme action. ✅

"🚨 Going limited under £50k might not save you tax.

At around £40,000 profit, the tax you pay as a sole trader can be very similar to running a limited company once you factor in corporation tax and dividend tax.

That’s why going limited isn’t always about tax savings. It can help you look more established, protect your personal assets, and make sense if you’re planning to grow past £50k profit.

💡 Bottom line: run the numbers before you switch.

Use it before April 5th… or lose it. ⏳💸

Your ISA (£20,000), £3,000 capital gains allowance, £500 dividend allowance, and up to £60,000 pension contribution all reset when the tax year ends. If you don’t use them, they don’t roll over, they disappear.

A higher-rate taxpayer could save up to £4,000 in tax for every £10,000 put into a pension. That’s money back in your pocket. Don’t let HMRC keep what you could keep.

column2: 6 Things That Could Trigger an HMRC Investigation
column7:
Could HMRC investigate you? Be prepared with these 6 tips 🔎

Receiving notice of an HMRC investigation can feel stressful and confusing, but it’s not as uncommon as you might think. Whether you’re an individual taxpayer or a business owner, it’s important to understand why these investigations happen and how to handle them confidently.

1. Discrepancies in Tax Returns
2. Unexplained Wealth or Lifestyle
3. Cash Transactions
4. Incomplete or Inaccurate Record-Keeping
5. Connections to Offshore Accounts or Assets
6. Employment and Business Structure

If you are being investigated, do these 6 things! 👇

1. Understand the Reason for the Investigation
2. Gather All Relevant Records
3️. Seek Professional Advice
4️. Be Transparent and Cooperative
5️. Review Your Filing Practices
6️. Stay Calm and Focused

column2: HMRC don't care what you claim as an expense
column7:
HMRC don’t care what you claim ❌

They care about why you’re claiming it ✅

To ensure your expenses align with their guidelines, consider these five key principles:

1️⃣ Business Use Only
Expenses must be used entirely and exclusively for your business operations. If an expense is purely business-related, it can be claimed.

2️⃣ Avoiding Mixed Purposes
If an expense has both business and personal benefits, it falls under scrutiny. You’ll need to demonstrate that any personal benefit is incidental to its business purpose.

3️⃣ Perks and Extras
Non-cash perks, like company benefits, can be claimed if the proper taxes are applied. These extras, often called benefits in kind, require careful handling to meet HMRC standards.

4️⃣ Spotless Records
Accurate and organized documentation is a must. Keep receipts, invoices, and records securely stored, and ensure personal and business finances are clearly separated.

5️⃣ The Small Stuff Counts
For smaller perks, trivial benefits might apply. These tax-free benefits must be under £X, not cash or cash-like, unrelated to performance rewards, and not part of a contract.

By following these principles, you’ll not only meet HMRC’s standards but also keep your claims worry-free.

5 Tax Hacks Everyone in the UK Should Know By 18
I know, I know. Taxes are stressful and boring.
But there’s nothing worse than losing your hard-earned money.
A few simple tips can completely streamline your finances, freeing up your time and energy.
Here’s what you need to do
 Save this for tax season!
 Know Your Personal Allowance
This is the amount you can earn tax free. For most, it’s £12,570, but it can vary.
 Save Money with Allowable Expenses
Did you know you can claim back costs for things like work related travel, tools, or even some home office expenses? These deductions can save you hundreds of pounds!
 Track All Sources of Income
Whether it’s from a side hustle, investments, or rental income, declare it all. Missing income can lead to penalties later.
 Meet Deadlines or Face Penalties
Missing the 31st January tax deadline can cost you up to £100 or more, even if you don’t owe tax.
Put the dates on your calendar!

1️⃣ “Deductions increase audit risk.” - False. Keep records tidy and you’re fine.
2️⃣ “Overseas income or crypto gains don’t count.” - Wrong. They’re taxable.
3️⃣ “Filing late is fine if I owe nothing.” - Nope. HMRC fines you anyway.
4️⃣ “Tax refunds are always good.” - Actually means you’ve been overpaying.

"By 18, You Should Know These Tax Hacks :saluting_face:
Most people don’t learn this in school… but knowing just a few tax basics can save you thousands over a lifetime.
:pushpin: Save this for later!
:one: The Government Tax Website Is Shutting Down:
:point_right:HMRC’s Government Gateway, where most people file taxes, is closing in 18 months :flushed:
:point_right:That means you’ll need to switch to third-party software to file your return.
:loudspeaker:Don’t wait until the last minute!
:two: Not Everyone Needs to File… But Many Do:
:point_right:Earning under £12,570? You might not need to file… but :eyes:
:point_right:If you’re self-employed, you still need to report your income.
:point_right:If you rent out property, earn dividends, or have a side hustle, HMRC needs to know.
:thought_balloon:They won’t remind you, it’s on you to check and file on time!
:three: Always Check Your Tax Code:
:point_right:Your tax code decides how much you actually take home :moneybag:
:point_right:A wrong tax code = overpaying or owing money :grimacing:
:point_right:If you’ve had multiple jobs or been on emergency tax, HMRC might owe you a refund :bulb:
:loudspeaker:You can check & update it online in minutes.
:four: Missing the Deadline = Automatic Fines
:point_right:Even if you owe £0 in tax, filing late = a fine :money_with_wings:
:point_right:First fine: £100 :rotating_light:
:point_right:After 3 months: £10 per day (up to £900!) :flushed:
:point_right:After 6 months: 5% of what you owe :grimacing:
:hourglass_flowing_sand: Set a reminder. Self-assessment deadline was 31st January!
:five: Don’t Assume You Can’t Claim Expenses
:point_right:If you work from home, drive for work, or buy tools/software, you can claim tax deductions :briefcase:
:point_right:But HMRC needs proof. So keep receipts, invoices, or bank statements :file_folder:
 :weary:No proof? No deduction.
Taxes are complicated… but HMRC won’t remind you of what you’re missing :eyes:
The more you know, the more money stays in YOUR pocket :dart:
:point_down: Comment ""TAX"" if you want to stay ahead of the game!

Most people focus on their salary… but not enough people look at what they’re actually taking home. 👀
Let’s break it down:

If you’re earning £70,000, you could be paying over £21,000 in Income Tax and National Insurance combined.
At £80,000? That’s roughly £25,000+ gone.
At £90,000? Around £30,000 in deductions.

And at £100,000? You’re looking at approximately £34,000 paid in Income Tax and NI.
That’s not small change, that’s house deposits, investments, holidays, and financial freedom.

The truth is, earning more doesn’t automatically mean keeping more. Without smart tax planning, higher income can simply mean higher deductions.

The good news? There are completely legal, smart ways to become more tax efficient, from pension contributions to salary sacrifice strategies and better financial structuring.

Don’t just work hard for your money.
Make your money work smarter for you. 💼💰

"Everyone’s talking about it… “high taxes are driving people abroad.” But is that really the full story? 
Yes, places like Dubai sound tempting with tax-friendly policies, but let’s be real: moving isn’t just about numbers on paper. Family, homes, businesses, and long-held assets aren’t things you can just pack into a suitcase overnight.
And when policies target wealth and not just income, it’s even harder to simply “leave.” Think about it, large property holdings, legacy assets, deep-rooted investments… those don’t just disappear.
So while some may explore options globally, not everyone is rushing for the exit. The conversation is more nuanced than headlines make it seem.
What do you think, would higher taxes actually make you move countries? "

Earning more should feel like progress, but for many people, it’s starting to feel like a penalty. With tax thresholds not keeping up with rising salaries and inflation, more people are quietly being pushed into higher tax brackets. This “fiscal drag” means your income goes up, but so does the chunk that goes to taxes.

The bigger conversation? Whether the system itself needs a reset. Higher thresholds and smarter tax structures could encourage people to earn, invest, and move forward financially instead of feeling stuck. Is it time to rethink how taxes work?

💬 Comment the word 'podcast' below and I'll send you link to full podcast.

#TaxNews #TaxReform #FiscalDrag #PersonalFinance #TaxTips

Raising taxes sounds like the easy answer… but what if it’s actually holding growth back? 🤔

Instead of increasing the burden, what if taxes were redistributed, giving some people a break, letting businesses grow, and then seeing what actually works?

The goal isn’t more pressure, it’s smarter systems. If growth happens, everyone wins. And if it doesn’t? Adjust, don’t repeat what’s already failing. 📊📉

Sometimes the problem isn’t how much we tax, it’s how we tax.

column2: Let's get rid of the jargon
column7:
📊 PAYE vs. Self-Assessment: What’s the difference?
Let’s break it down (without the jargon):

👔 PAYE
You’re employed by someone. Your employer handles the tax bit — it comes out of your wages before you even see them. Easy peasy.
But! You could still be owed tax back if you’ve:
– 🛠 Paid for your own tools or uniform
– 🏡 Worked from home
– 🚆 Travelled for work
– 🔄 Changed jobs partway through a tax year

🧾 Self-Assessment
You’re responsible for reporting your own income (think freelancers, contractors, side hustlers). No one’s doing it for you.
That means:
– 📈 You track your income & expenses
– 🧮 You file a tax return every year
– 💸 You might need to pay in advance for next year (cheeky, we know)

💡 Key pointers
If you’re PAYE, tax’s done for you — but if you’re due a tax refund, you’ll need to claim it yourself.

column2: Myth: You don’t need to file if you earned under £1,000.
column7:
"❌ “My income was under £1,000, so I don’t need to file a tax return... right?”
👉 Not so fast!

Even if you made less than £1,000 from self-employment, you may still need to submit a tax return if:
✅ You’re a company director
✅ You had to pay Capital Gains Tax on a gain made in the year
✅ You earned untaxed income (e.g. rental income, tips, crypto gains)
✅ You received income from abroad
✅ You or your partner claim Child Benefit and your income is over £50,000
✅ You’re self-employed and want to pay voluntary Class 2 NICs to protect your state pension

💡 Bottom line? Don’t assume—check the HMRC criteria.
📌 Save this post. It could save you a fine."

"❌ “I don’t need to declare my crypto gains unless I cash out to GBP…”
Think again 👇

💰 HMRC treats crypto as an asset, not cash.
That means you're potentially liable for Capital Gains Tax when you:
🔁 Swap one crypto for another (e.g. ETH → SOL)
🎁 Gift crypto (unless it’s to your spouse)
🛒 Spend crypto on goods or services
💼 Sell crypto for fiat (GBP or other)

Even if your bank account was never touched, HMRC still wants to know.
🧾 Keep detailed records of every transaction.

Tax on crypto is real. Don’t risk a penalty by ignoring it.

column2:
"PENSION DEATH BENEFITS TO BE TAXED?
 CHANGES COMING IN 2027 "
column7:
📢 NEW IHT RULES ANNOUNCED FOR PENSION DEATH BENEFITS, STARTING APRIL 2027
Unused pension funds and death benefits under registered pension schemes will come within scope of Inheritance Tax (IHT) from 6 April 2027.
Here’s what you really need to know:
 🔹 Not everything is affected, Death in service benefits (e.g. NHS Pension Scheme) are excluded
 🔹 Spouses & civil partners are exempt (same as general IHT rules)
 🔹 IHT will now be the responsibility of the deceased’s personal representatives (PRs)
 🔹 Beneficiaries will be jointly liable for paying IHT (yes, really)
 🔹 You can ask the pension scheme to pay the IHT directly before you receive the benefit
But here's the kicker...
 If someone dies after age 75, both IHT and income tax could apply.
 ☠️ That’s a total tax rate of up to 67% on your inheritance.
So what does this mean for you?
 If you plan to pass down unused pensions, you need to review your estate planning now, and your loved ones should understand what’s coming.
This change is real, complex, and carries financial risk, but with knowledge comes power.
🧠 Knowledge is power, especially with tax.
 📥 Save & share to keep others informed.

column7:
Most people underestimate just how much they’ll need for retirement. The Rule of 25 is a simple way to calculate it:
👉 Take your annual expenses and multiply by 25. That’s the size of investment pot you’ll need to sustain your lifestyle.
It’s based on the “4% Rule”, the idea that you can safely withdraw 4% of your investments each year without running out of money.
For example:
Want £40k a year in retirement? You’ll need roughly £1m invested.


Want £80k a year? You’ll need around £2m.


Sounds daunting? Here’s the truth ⤵️
 💡 The earlier you start planning, the less painful it feels. Smart use of pensions, ISAs, and tax-efficient planning can shrink the gap.
This is why filing and organising your self-assessment properly matters, it’s not just about paying HMRC, it’s about freeing up more to invest for your future.
Would you rather retire earlier with less, or work longer and have more freedom later? 🤔

column2: Does your Investing account match your goal?
column7:
💡 Same £200 a month. Different account. Totally different outcomes.

Here’s why:
 🔹 Stocks & Shares ISA - flexible, no tax on withdrawals, but no employer match.
 🔹 Personal Pension - gets tax relief upfront, but 75% is taxed when you take it out.
 🔹 Workplace Pension - same as personal pension plus employer contributions = the biggest boost.
👉 The numbers suggest pensions grow bigger, but ISAs win on flexibility.
👉 And with workplace pensions, the employer match is often too good to ignore.
There’s no single “best” option, and it depends on your situation, tax rate, and how much flexibility you want. ⚖️

column7:
We’re told the magic number for a “comfortable” retirement is £800,000.
 That’s the size of pension pot supposedly needed to produce around £44k per year according to the Retirement Living Standards report.
Not exactly pocket change.
But here’s the twist hardly anyone is acknowledging 
From 6 April 2027, if you don’t use all of that pension during your lifetime, the part left behind might not pass fully intact to your children.
 It could actually be hit with 40% Inheritance Tax.
And yes, that sounds wild, considering it’s money you built up for decades.
Here’s how the rules break down:
 If you’re unmarried:
You can pass on £325k tax-free.
 Everything above that = 40% tax.
 If you’re married:
You can combine allowances for £650k tax-free.
 AND your main home matters too:
Leave your primary residence to your children =

Building your credit score in the Uk involves a series of responsible financial practices that demonstrate your reliability to lenders.
1. Register on the electoral roll:- Being on the electoral register helps lenders verify your identity and address.

2. open a Uk bank account:- Having a bank account helps establish a financial footprint in the UK. Ensure you manage it well.

3. Apply first a credit card:- If you are new to credit, apply first a credit card designed for people with no or limited credit history. These typically have higher interest rates. Keep your balance low and aim to pay it off in full each month. This shows lenders that you can manage credit responsibly.

4. Rake out a small loan:- A personal loan or even a phone contract helps build your credit history if you keep up with payments. Only borrow what you can afford to repay.

5. Maintains a low credit utilization rate:- Keep your credit utilization below 30% .

6. Set Up direct debits for payments:- Missed or late payment harm your credit score. Set up direct debits for bills such as phone contracts, utilities or loan repayments to ensure you never miss a payment.

7. Avoid multiple credit applications:- Every time you apply for credit, a hard search appears on your report, which can negatively impact your credit score.

8. Check your credit report regularly.

9. Keep old credit accounts open:- The longer your credit history, the better. If you have old credit cards that ate paid off, it may be wise to keep them open to show a long history of credit use.

10. Avoid default:- missing payments on loans or bills can lead to default.

11. Stay within your credit limits:- Avoid exceeding your cred limits. Staying within limits demonstrates responsible borrowing.

A £60k salary brings home £45,357 a year after income tax and National Insurance, about £3,780 per month. Two earners at £60k take home roughly £7,560 combined.
At £120k, the personal allowance is tapered down to £2,570. After income tax and NI, take-home pay is around £78,157 per year, roughly £6,513 per month.
That’s a real gap of about £1,047 per month, even though both households earn the same £120,000 gross.
Now add policy design.
Child benefit for two children is £26.05 per week for the first child and £17.25 for the second, £2,251.60 per year.
But the High Income Child Benefit Charge starts once one individual earns over £60,000, and it’s fully withdrawn by £80,000. It’s based on the highest individual earner, not total household income.
So two parents earning £60k each keep the full £2,251.60.
A single parent earning £120k loses it all.
The same cliff-edge logic applies to 30 hours free childcare. If either parent earns over £100k individually, eligibility disappears entirely. A couple on £99k each (nearly £200k combined) can qualify. A single parent on £105k cannot.
This isn’t about exaggerating numbers.
The maths holds.
The UK taxes individuals.
It withdraws support using individual thresholds.
But families live on household income.
That’s the distortion.
And that’s why it feels broken.

Want to legally pay less tax this year? Here are some personal allowances many people forget to use, and they could help you save more of your income.

£12,570 Personal Allowance
If your income is under this threshold, you pay zero income tax.

If you earn from a side hustle and it’s under £1,000, you usually don’t even need to report it.

Small amounts of rental or property income under £1,000 can be tax-free.

Interest earned up to £1,000 can be tax-free.

Higher rate taxpayers can still earn some savings interest tax-free.

£3,000 Capital Gains Annual Exempt Amount
If your capital gains are under £3,000 in a tax year, you may not have to pay Capital Gains Tax.

These allowances are built into the tax system, but many people don’t fully use them. Understanding them can help you keep more of your money.

:gb: Leaving the UK permanently? Can you get your TAX, NI & pension money back? :eyes:

Everyone thinks the UK keeps your money forever… but that’s not fully true. Here’s the real talk :point_down:

:money_with_wings: TAX REFUND:
If you leave mid-tax year (April–April), you might get some tax back. You tell HMRC you’re leaving and they calculate if you overpaid. Many people actually get a refund.

:x: NATIONAL INSURANCE (NI):
This one hurts. You usually CANNOT get NI back. It’s not savings, it funds NHS, benefits, and pensions. Once paid, it’s gone.

:older_adult: STATE PENSION:
Good news, you don’t lose it.
If you worked enough years, you can still get a UK pension even if you live abroad forever.
10 years = minimum pension
35 years = full pension
(Some countries don’t get yearly increases though.)

:office: WORKPLACE / PRIVATE PENSION:
You usually can’t refund it after 2 years.
You can leave it in the UK, transfer it abroad, or take it at retirement age.

:airplane: Coming back later?
You can return to the UK, work, and your NI years still count. Nothing resets.

Save this if you’re planning to move abroad :point_down:

HMRC won't shout about these.. but here are some legit expense claims UK self-employed people often miss :point_down:

:sparkles: Professional insurance
If you pay for insurance wholly for your business, it’s usually allowable.
Examples include:
• Public liability insurance
• Professional indemnity insurance
• Employer’s liability (if you have staff)
• Insurance for business equipment or tools
Personal policies like life insurance or income protection usually aren’t allowable.

:sparkles: Marketing & advertising
You can claim costs for promoting your business, such as:
• online ads (Google / social media)
• website hosting & domain names
• business cards, flyers, brochures
• branding or design services

:sparkles: Business software & subscriptions
Subscriptions used to run your business are usually allowable, for example:
• accounting software
• design or editing software
• project management tools
• cloud storage or email systems
If something is used for both personal and business use, only the business portion can be claimed.

:sparkles: Business travel
You can claim travel wholly for business journeys, including:
• train, bus, or taxi fares
• parking fees and congestion charges
• mileage if using your own vehicle
• flights for business trips
Overnight business trips can also include hotel stays and reasonable meals.
Travel between home and a regular permanent workplace isn’t allowable.

:sparkles: Bank fees & loan interest
You can claim:
• bank charges on a business account
• payment processing fees (PayPal, Stripe etc.)
• interest on business loans or overdrafts
The loan repayment itself isn’t an expense, only the interest is.

:sparkles: Working from home (simplified method)
If you work from home, HMRC allows a flat monthly claim based on hours worked:
• 25–50 hours → £10/month
• 51–100 hours → £18/month
• 101+ hours → £26/month

This covers heat, electricity and water.

Phone and internet can be claimed separately based on business use.

Want your house to pass to your kids without a huge inheritance tax bill?

 Here are some key rules most people get wrong :point_down:

:one: Spouse rule
If you own your home jointly with your spouse as joint tenants, it usually passes to them automatically.

 :point_right: But if you own as tenants in common, your share follows your will instead.

:two: The 40% tax myth
 Inheritance tax is 40%, but only on the part of your estate above £325,000, not simply “what your child inherits.”

:three: The £500K allowance (big one people miss)
 You may be able to pass on up to £500,000 tax-free if:

You leave your home to your children, and
It qualifies as your main residence


:bulb: Married couples can potentially pass on up to £1 million tax-free.

:warning: But this allowance reduces if your estate is worth over £2 million.

:four: Gifting your house (danger zone :rotating_light:)

 Yes,  if you gift your house and live 7 years, it can fall outside your estate.

BUT…
 :x: If you still live in the house rent-free, it likely still counts for tax

 (This is called a “gift with reservation of benefit”, and it catches a lot of people out.)

Small details make a big difference when it comes to inheritance tax.

Most sole traders don't realise this until it's too late, HMRC taxes you on invoices you've raised, not always on money you've actually been paid. And if a client never pays you, you could still owe tax on that income. :flushed:

Here's everything you need to know :arrow_down:

How sole trader tax works by default:
HMRC uses the "arising basis", meaning income is taxable in the tax year the work was done and invoiced, not necessarily when the cash lands in your account.

So if you invoiced a client £5,000 in March 2026 and they didn't pay until May 2026, that income still sits in your 2025–26 tax year. You owe tax on it in January 2027, even if the money arrived months later.

What happens if a client never pays you?
If a debt becomes genuinely irrecoverable, the client has gone bust, disappeared, or formally disputed, you can claim it as a bad debt expense on your Self Assessment.

But you must be able to prove: :white_check_mark: The debt genuinely existed (invoice evidence) :white_check_mark: You took reasonable steps to recover it :white_check_mark: It is genuinely irrecoverable, not just late

You cannot claim a bad debt simply because a client is slow to pay.

The cash basis, a simpler alternative:

Sole traders with turnover under £150,000 can elect to use cash basis accounting instead. Under cash basis:


You only pay tax on money actually received
You only claim expenses when actually paid
Bad debts are not an issue, because you never counted unpaid invoices as income


For most straightforward sole traders this is simpler and avoids the problem entirely. You elect for cash basis on your Self Assessment return.

Why this matters:

Imagine invoicing heavily in February and March. the final weeks of the tax year. All of that income lands in your 2025–26 tax bill. But the cash might not arrive until April or May.

You owe HMRC before the money even reaches your account.
Plan your invoicing and cash flow around your tax bill, not just your bank balance.

Are you on cash basis or traditional accounting?

Most sole traders don't even know which one they're using.

Check your last Self Assessment return.
